ecosystem: mountain lake (primary); cold mountain streams; coastal rivers

taxonomy.note: FINAL Salmonidae entry in engine (arc 236-242). Species epithet 'clarkii' honors William Clark of the Lewis and Clark Expedition. ~14 recognized subspecies, most taxonomically complex native PNW salmonid.

washington_subspecies.name: Coastal Cutthroat
washington_subspecies.range: Coastal streams, mountain lakes, sea-run form

wa_state_fish.note: Washington State Fish since 1969. Cultural centerpiece of PNW freshwater angling.

lewis_clark_connection.notes: First scientific documentation in western science. Clark described 'red streaks' along jaw and 'remarkably fine flavour.'

sound_profile.energy: E=2 -- the delicate acoustic signature of a trout feeding at the surface; the primary auditory cue in PNW mountain lake flyfishing

ecology.spawning: Spring (March-June), tributary streams, clean gravel redds
ecology.ecological_role: Apex predator in mountain lake ecosystem; top-down regulator of invertebrate community

conservation.primary_threat: Rainbow Trout (O. mykiss) hybridization producing 'Cuttbow' hybrids; replaces pure populations over generations
secondary_threats: Yellowstone Lake: Lake Trout introduction caused Cutthroat collapse

theory_nodes.concept: FINAL SALMONIDAE in engine. Arc 236-242 closes. Seven consecutive degrees of salmon, trout, char. Arc began with Pacific Salmon's loud spawning runs (E=5); closes with Cutthroat's quiet surface rise (E=2). Beginning and end within the same family.

Lewis and Clark first description 1805: William Clark documented Oncorhynchus clarkii in the upper Missouri drainage. Species epithet 'clarkii' permanently honors Clark's role. Formal naming by Charles Girard 1856. One of the few PNW species precisely dated to a specific historical event.
theory_nodes.description: Lewis and Clark as founding moment for scientific documentation of cutthroat.

theory_nodes.concept: The surface rise as the iconic sound of PNW flyfishing. E=2 acoustic profile: the sip or gentle plop of a feeding trout in still mountain water. In near-silence, audible from 20-30m. The acoustic cue that precedes the cast.
theory_nodes.description: Surface rise as acoustic signature and cultural artifact.

cross_references.relationship: Final two Salmonidae: both E=2, both quiet cold-water species, both under pressure from introduced fish. Consecutive placement closes the char/trout section.
Arc bookends: Pacific Salmon (E=5, spawning splash) opens Salmonidae; Cutthroat (E=2, surface rise) closes it. Same family, opposite acoustic profiles.

The species immediately following. Transition from Salmonidae (242) to Petromyzontidae (243) -- the most dramatic taxonomic jump in SPS. From elegant native trout to 450-million-year-old jawless fish.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=7 vs E=2. The original PNW rock band (Wailers, 1959) against the original PNW native trout (Cutthroat, state fish since 1969). Both 'original natives' of their respective domains.
